expectations.text: The document is complete to the skill's template: all 15 sections are present and filled, and the Decision Coverage Checklist accounts for every one of the 22 taxonomy dimensions (A1-A14 and B1-B8) with a status (Resolved / Assumed / N/A) and a substantive note — no row left blank or rubber-stamped.
expectations.evidence: Sections 1–15 are all present and substantively filled. Section 15 (Decision Coverage Checklist) lists all 22 rows: A1–A14 and B1–B8, each with a status (Resolved, Assumed, or N/A) and a specific cross-reference note, e.g. "A3 Concurrency & consistency | Resolved (D1) | §7, D1 — unique constraint on (user_id, iso_week) is the concurrency control" and "A14 Accessibility & device/env | N/A | Email rendering is handled by email client; HTML email accessibility is a template concern, out of scope for this design."

expectations.text: Assumptions and Accepted Compromises are each first-class tables (not prose); every accepted compromise names the specific design principle it bends and gives a concrete revisit trigger.
expectations.evidence: Section 11 is a table with columns #, Assumption, Why safe to assume, Needs confirmation? Section 12 is a table with columns #, Compromise, What we give up, Principle bent, Why acceptable now, Revisit trigger. Every row names the principle bent (e.g. C1: "Principle 14: Least surprise", C2: "Principle 5: Design for failure (full recovery)") and a concrete revisit trigger (e.g. C1: "If business requires SLA-level delivery time guarantees", C3: "If the security model is elevated (e.g., compliance requirements) or tokens must be revocable").

expectations.text: The idempotency mechanism guarantees one digest per user per week even under a concurrent double-run of the job — it relies on a database-enforced dedup (e.g. a unique key on user + week), not a check-then-send that has a race window. (Correctness trap: an application-level 'already sent?' check is not concurrency-safe.)
expectations.evidence: D1 states: "Before doing any work, DigestUserJob attempts to insert a row into digest_sends(user_id, iso_week). A UNIQUE(user_id, iso_week) constraint makes a second insert fail atomically. The job treats a unique-violation as success and exits immediately." The schema confirms: "CONSTRAINT digest_sends_user_week_unique UNIQUE (user_id, iso_week)". The design explicitly rejects a flag on the user row: "A flag on the user row would create a race between two concurrent scheduler runs; the insert-or-skip pattern is atomic."

expectations.text: The design handles the email-provider failure rule correctly: retries are time-bounded, and on continued failure the digest is dropped for the week rather than delayed — and the design explicitly ties this to the blueprint's 'a late digest is worse than a skipped one' rule.
expectations.evidence: D5: "On failure, Sidekiq retries up to 3 times with exponential backoff (e.g., 15s, 60s, 240s). If all retries fail, Sidekiq calls the sidekiq_retries_exhausted callback, which: Updates digest_sends.status = 'dropped'... The user receives no email for the week. No late delivery is attempted. This matches the explicit blueprint decision: late > skip is false." Section 7 also states: "The user receives no email for the week. No late delivery is attempted. This matches the explicit blueprint decision: late > skip is false."

expectations.text: Per-user-timezone send scheduling is designed concretely (how 8am-local across many timezones becomes scheduled sends), and the design handles the blueprint's 'job runs late' deviation without corrupting the seven-day content window.
expectations.evidence: D3 concretely describes: "DigestSchedulerJob runs every hour on Monday (via Sidekiq-Cron). On each run, it identifies UTC offsets for which 8 AM local falls within the current UTC hour, then queries users in those timezones." For the late-run deviation, Section 7 states: "The seven-day window is computed at the time the DigestUserJob runs (not when enqueued), based on the user's local Monday 00:00. A delay in job start does not affect window content — only send time slips."

expectations.text: The design specifies that no email is sent when a user has no relevant activity, and correctly handles the mid-week new-user partial window — both blueprint rules.
expectations.evidence: D4: "DigestUserJob queries the activity, and if the result set is empty (no new items, completed items, or mentions in the window), it marks the digest_sends row as skipped and exits without calling SendGrid." D8: "The digest window lower bound is MAX(Monday_00:00_local - 7 days, user.created_at). The query always uses the user's account creation date as the floor." The blueprint coverage checklist confirms: "Brand-new user joined mid-week | Edge case | D8 | Window lower bound is MAX(Monday - 7 days, user.created_at)"

expectations.text: The unsubscribe link is designed to be unguessable and scoped to the issuing account (a signed or random token, not a sequential identifier), addressing the adversarial scenario; and digest content is scoped to only what each user is permitted to see.
expectations.evidence: D6: "The token is HMAC-SHA256(secret_key, 'unsubscribe:{user_id}:{iso_week}') encoded as URL-safe Base64... UnsubscribeController validates the HMAC, extracts user_id from the payload... An invalid HMAC returns HTTP 400; no information about the user is leaked." D7: "The activity query for each user is scoped by a permission filter... The permission filter must be applied in the query, not post-hoc in the assembler, to avoid fetching and discarding data the user was never supposed to see."

expectations.text: The design names the central scheduling trade-off and resolves it with principle-grounded reasoning, and the digest-assembly query is bounded — the 'up to ten highlighted items, +N more' cap is reflected as a real query limit, not unbounded fetching.
expectations.evidence: D3 names and resolves the scheduling trade-off: fan-out hourly bucketing vs. single Monday 00:00 job with per-user delays vs. one job per timezone offset, resolved with Principle 1 (Simplicity first) and Principle 14 (Least surprise). The query is bounded with a real LIMIT: §6 states "Each query scans a bounded window (7 days) with a LIMIT 10 for highlighted items" and the Blueprint Coverage Checklist row explicitly shows "ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 10 in query; assembler adds '+N more' count if total_count > 10."
